elements.sent-2411-001: This dissertation is my own work and contains nothing which is the outcome of work done in collaboration with others, except as specified in the text and Acknowledgements.
elements.sent-2411-002: This dissertation contains fewer than 65,000 words including appendices, bibliography, footnotes, tables and equations and has fewer than 150 figures.. \end{declaration.}
elements.sent-2411-003: Introduction Hyperkinetic movement disorders (HMDs) in children mainly comprise dystonia (abnormal twisting) and chorea (irregular, random movements).
elements.sent-2411-004: HMDs present significant diagnostic challenges due to their complex and often overlapping clinical manifestations.
elements.sent-2411-005: Prevalence of these conditions ranges from 2 to 50 per million and 5 to 10 per 100,000 respectively.
elements.sent-2411-006: These patients typically experience diagnostic delays averaging 4.75 to 7.83 years.
elements.sent-2411-007: Traditional diagnostic methods rely on clinical history and physical examinations by an expert, as specialised tests do not improve detection due to their complex pathophysiology.
elements.sent-2411-008: To address challenges in diagnosis, this study develops a neural network model to differentiate between dystonia and chorea from video recordings of patients performing a specific task.
elements.sent-2411-009: Methods The model utilizes joint position data extracted from videos of paediatric patients performing motor tasks.
elements.sent-2411-010: The model architecture integrates a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN), to capture spatial relationships, and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), to represent temporal dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-011: Attention mechanisms were incorporated to refine feature extraction and improve the model’s interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-012: A dataset of 50 videos (31 chorea-predominant, 19 dystonia-predominant) was used to train and validate the model.
elements.sent-2411-013: All data was collected under regulatory approval by Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust (GSTT) Electronic Records Research Interface (GERRI) (REC\# 20EM/0112).
elements.sent-2411-014: Results and Discussion The model achieved an accuracy of 85\%, and an F1 score of 81\%.
elements.sent-2411-015: The model demonstrated high sensitivity (81\%) and specificity (88\%) at the optimal frame rate of 15 frames per second (fps).
elements.sent-2411-016: Attention maps provided valuable insights into the model's decision-making, highlighting the model’s correct identification of involuntary movement patterns, while model misclassifications are often linked to occluded body parts or subtle movement variations.
elements.sent-2411-017: This study highlights the potential of deep learning to improve the accuracy and efficiency of HMD diagnosis and ultimately could contribute to more reliable, interpretable clinical tools for paediatric HMDs.
